Toyota.”276 To judge from my own conversations, ordinary French people, who often

cite this case, seem to agree with them. There are other recent cases too, in which parents

have been prevented from giving their children names that are “ridiculous, pejorative or

in bad taste.”277 One Belgian woman, for example, was recently forbidden to name her

newborn “Anakin,” after the character in the Star Wars movie series. Despite her threat

to go on a hunger strike, officials decreed that her child was to be called “Dorian.”278

There is even European human rights law on the issue. The case in question involved a

French couple that chose to name their child “Fleur de Marie,” (“Mary’s Flower”) a name

276
    JCP 2001, IV.2655. The court’s opinon emphasized that the parents had had any “arrières-pensées”—
that is, any unacknowledged or ulterior intentions, and that the car model in question would likely go out of
production by the time the child reached school age.
277
    Discussion and citations in Méga Code Civil, 5th ed. (2003), commentary paragraph 3 to Art. 57, p. 177.
278
    Nach dem “Krieg der Sterne” Kampf un einen Vorname, Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung, January 18,
2000.

89
 rejected by local officials on the grounds that, since it was not a saints’ name, it was not

in the best interest of the child. That decision was litigated all the way the European

Court of Human Rights, which held, in 1996, over a dissent by an English judge, that the

law of names did not violate any right of privacy. 279

All very strange, for Americans. To be sure, the law of names has been loosening

up, both in France and Germany. French law has eased up noticeably since the early

1990s.280 In the last few years, cases have been few in France—though the standard

commentary to the Civil Code speculates that this may be because pre-litigation

interventions by officials are sufficient to discourage unacceptable names. 281 As for

Germany: There, the most important challenges to the law of names came from the many

resident non-Germans wishing to give their children ethnic names. The German

government responded essentially by extending its list to include acceptable names for all

recognized ethnic groups. These days, Germans can theoretically pick any name that

comes from some culture, as long as it appears in the official “International Handbook of

Given Names” and is spelled correctly. 282 (In my experience, though, few Germans

realize how much latitude they have.) At any rate, the European law of names is

certainly not applied in a doctrinaire or draconian way. It is complex body of law, in a

state of some flux, which deserves a longer treatment than I can give it here.
